function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
When evaluating future orbital AI compute satellites, which disclosed accounting policies or asset categories provide the closest precedent for treatment? Does the filing explicitly resolve whether such assets would be capitalized or expensed?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's S-1 filing and specifically about:
1. Which disclosed accounting policies or asset categories provide the closest precedent for treatment of future orbital AI compute satellites
2. Whether the filing explicitly resolves whether such assets would be capitalized or expensed

I need to parse the SpaceX S-1 filing and then retrieve information about accounting policies related to satellites, orbital assets, AI compute, and capitalization vs expensing policies.

Let me start by parsing the filing.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about accounting policies related to satellites, orbital assets, capitalization policies, and property/equipment treatment.

arguments.prompt: What are the accounting policies for satellite capitalization, property and equipment, and orbital assets in {{spacex_s1}}?
What accounting policies relate to AI compute, data center, or compute infrastructure assets in {{spacex_s1}}?
What are the disclosed asset categories for property, plant and equipment and how are satellites classified in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided financial statement chunks, here are the accounting policies regarding satellite capitalization, general property and equipment, and orbital/flight assets:

### 1. Satellite Capitalization and Accounting
*   **Costs Capitalized:** Satellite assets include the direct costs to build them (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as capitalized launch costs incurred to get them into orbit. 
*   **Inter-Segment Launches:** When the Space segment launches internal Starlink satellites, no inter-segment revenue is recognized. Instead, the launch costs (which include an allocation of flight vehicle costs) are directly capitalized into the satellite asset within Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E).
*   **Useful Life & Depreciation:** Satellites are depreciated using the **straight-line method**. Useful lives are estimated based on engineering studies, historical on-orbit performance, propellant life, utilization patterns, and planned technology transitions. 
    *   **Broadband satellites:** 5-year useful life.
    *   **First-generation mobile satellites:** 3-year useful life.
    *   **General Range:** 3 to 5 years.
*   **Sensitivity:** A one-year change in the average useful life of satellite assets would impact operating income by approximately $480 million (for the year ended Dec 31, 2025) and $170 million (for the three months ended Mar 31, 2026).

### 2. Property, Plant, and Equipment (General PP&E)
*   **Capitalization vs. Expensing:** Routine maintenance and repairs are expensed as incurred. Significant refurbishments, renewals, and enhancements that increase an asset's functionality, output, or expected life are capitalized and depreciated ratably over the identified useful life.
*   **Interest Capitalization:** The company capitalizes interest costs for significant long-term construction projects (such as AI infrastructure data centers and launch facilities). Capitalization begins when preparation activities commence, is calculated using average accumulated expenditures and the company's weighted average borrowing rate, and ceases when the asset is substantially completed. Capitalized interest becomes part of the asset's historical cost.
*   **Standard Useful Lives:** Depreciated primarily on a straight-line basis over the following estimated lives:
    *   **Servers and networking equipment:** 5 - 6 years
    *   **Machinery and equipment:** 3 - 10 years
    *   **Data center infrastructure:** 20 - 25 years
    *   **Launch sites:** 7 - 20 years
    *   **Buildings and improvements:** 30 years
    *   **Leasehold improvements:** Shorter of 7 - 20 years or the life of the lease
*   **Impairment & Disposals:** PP&E is evaluated for impairment at the lowest level of independent cash flows whenever events indicate the carrying value may not be recoverable. Routine disposals, scrapping, and gateway decommissions are charged to *Cost of Revenue* or *SG&A*. Abnormal impairments (e.g., damaged hardware, abandoned production lines, facility consolidations) are charged to *Impairment* or *Restructuring* expenses.

### 3. Orbital Assets and Flight Vehicles (Aerospace Hardware)
*   **Classification (Reusable vs. Expendable vs. Development):**
    *   **Reusable Hardware:** Boosters (first stages), fairings, Merlin engines, and Dragon capsules are classified as **PP&E**.
    *   **Non-Reusable (Expendable):** Second stages are not reusable; they are recorded as **Inventory** and expensed to *Cost of Revenue* upon launch or assignment.
    *   **Development Stage:** Starship (boosters, ships, and Raptor engines) is currently in development. The majority of these costs are expensed to *Research and Development* as incurred, and Raptor engines are expensed when used in test flights.
*   **Depreciation Method (Flight Cycles):** Unlike standard PP&E, reusable flight vehicles and spacecraft are depreciated based on the **expected number of average flights** (ranging from 5 to 25 flights) rather than a time-based straight-line method.
*   **Estimating Flight Lives:** Management periodically reviews and estimates the number of flights a vehicle can complete based on:
    1.  Recovery and refurbishment success rates and economic feasibility (supported by declining refurbishment costs).
    2.  Customer acceptance limits for reflown hardware.
    3.  Expected future launches in the mission manifest and anticipated retirement timing (e.g., transitioning from Falcon to Starship).
*   **Sensitivity:** A change of five more or fewer average remaining flights for flight vehicles and spacecraft would *not* have a material impact on the company's operating income.

Based on the provided financial statement chunks, the accounting policies specifically relating to AI compute, data centers, and compute infrastructure assets are detailed across several areas, including Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E), Leases, and Income Statement classification. 

Here are the specific accounting policies that apply to these assets:

### 1. Depreciation and Estimated Useful Lives
The company depreciates its AI and data center hardware and infrastructure over specific estimated useful lives:
*   **Servers and networking equipment:** 5 to 6 years.
*   **Data center infrastructure:** 20 to 25 years.

### 2. Construction-in-Progress (CIP) and Interest Capitalization
*   **Construction-in-Progress:** CIP primarily consists of ongoing construction and expansion of facilities, as well as **AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service**.
*   **Capitalized Interest:** Interest is capitalized during the construction period for significant long-term construction projects. The policy explicitly includes **AI infrastructure data centers** as qualifying assets for interest capitalization (e.g., $169 million capitalized for the year ended December 31, 2025, and $7 million for the three months ended March 31, 2026).

### 3. Lease Accounting for Data Centers and AI Hardware
The company leases data centers, facilities, and AI infrastructure hardware (such as the $6.58 billion equipment lease with Valor) under the following policies:
*   **Lease Classification:** Leases with contractual terms longer than 12 months are classified as either operating or finance leases at inception. Finance leases are used if the company substantially utilizes or pays for the entire asset over its estimated life.
*   **Short-Term Leases:** Leases with terms of 12 months or less are not recorded on the consolidated balance sheets and are expensed on a straight-line basis.
*   **Initial Measurement:** Upon commencement, a lease liability is recognized and measured at the present value of fixed payments. Renewal or early termination options are only included in the lease term if they are reasonably certain to be exercised.

### 4. Impairment and Restructuring
*   **Facilities Consolidation:** Impairment charges related to leasehold improvements and office equipment resulting from facility consolidation efforts specifically within the **AI segment** are recorded in **Restructuring charges** on the consolidated statements of operations (e.g., $36 million in 2024 and $54 million in 2023).
*   **General Impairment:** Assets are subject to impairment if considered outside the normal course of business, though no general PP&E impairment was recorded for the AI segment in the most recent periods presented.

### 5. Expense Classification (Cost of Revenue vs. R&D)
The company has distinct policies for how ongoing compute and data center operational costs are recognized on the income statement:
*   **Cost of Revenue (AI Segment):** Includes infrastructure costs required to run and serve the AI models. This encompasses data center facility costs (leases, hosting, support, maintenance, energy, and bandwidth), **depreciation of servers and networking equipment**, public cloud hosting costs, and related employee compensation.
*   **Research & Development (AI Segment):** Includes data center costs and cloud computing expenses that are directly tied to the **training of frontier AI models** (like Grok) and the development/build/testing of next-generation AI-enabled products.

Based on the provided financial statements and notes, here are the disclosed asset categories for Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E) and the specific classifications and accounting treatments for satellites.

### **Disclosed Asset Categories for PP&E**
The balance sheet notes (Note 5) break down Property, Plant, and Equipment, net into the following specific categories:
*   Servers and networking equipment
*   **Satellites**
*   Machinery and equipment
*   Data center infrastructure
*   Launch sites
*   Land, buildings and improvements
*   Flight vehicle hardware
*   Leasehold improvements
*   Construction-in-progress

---

### **How Satellites are Classified and Accounted For**
Satellites are classified and managed across several dimensions, including balance sheet presentation, cost capitalization, useful life estimates, and generational technology types:

**1. Balance Sheet & Segment Classification**
*   **PP&E Line Item:** Satellites are classified as a distinct, standalone asset category within "Property, plant, and equipment, net."
*   **Operating Segment:** They are primarily associated with the **Connectivity segment** (which operates the Starlink network). For example, impairment charges for damaged satellite hardware are recorded against the Connectivity segment.

**2. Cost Composition & Capitalization**
*   **Build & Launch Costs:** The "Satellites" asset category includes the costs to build the satellites (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as **capitalized launch costs** incurred by the Space segment to put them into orbit (which includes an allocation of flight vehicle costs).
*   **Inter-segment Elimination:** When the Space segment launches Starlink satellites for the Connectivity segment, the company does not recognize inter-segment revenue. Instead, those launch costs are directly capitalized into the "Satellites" PP&E account. 

**3. Useful Life & Depreciation**
*   **Estimated Useful Life:** Satellites are depreciated over an estimated useful life of **3 to 5 years**, based on engineering studies, on-orbit performance, propellant life, and technology evolution.
*   **Specific Classifications by Use-Case:**
    *   **Broadband satellites:** Estimated useful life of **5 years**.
    *   **First-generation mobile satellites:** Estimated useful life of **3 years**.

**4. Generational / Technical Classification (Prospectus Summary)**
From a product and operational standpoint, the company classifies its satellite constellations into specific generations based on their technical capabilities:
*   **V1 Mobile satellites:** Currently in orbit; provide light data, SMS, and over-the-top voice services to mobile devices.
*   **V2 Mini satellites:** Current broadband satellites providing high-speed internet to homes, businesses, and vehicles.
*   **V2 Mobile satellites:** Next-generation mobile satellites designed for comprehensive satellite-to-mobile services (broadband data and IoT).
*   **V3 satellites:** Next-generation Starlink broadband satellites offering 1 Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite (expected to deploy on Starship).

steps.reasoning_content: Good, I have substantial information. Now let me search for any specific mention of "orbital AI compute satellites" or any forward-looking statements about AI compute in orbit, or any discussion of how such assets would be treated.

arguments.prompt: Does the filing mention orbital AI compute satellites, space-based AI compute, or AI compute satellites in orbit in {{spacex_s1}}?
What are the research and development capitalization policies, software development costs, and internally developed technology accounting in {{spacex_s1}}?
What forward-looking statements or risk factors relate to future satellite types, new satellite constellations, or planned orbital infrastructure in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Yes, the filing explicitly mentions **orbital AI compute satellites**, **space-based AI compute** (and related terms like "space-based infrastructure" and "Space based compute"), and **AI compute satellites in orbit** (e.g., "AI compute satellites in Sun-synchronous orbit"). 

These concepts are detailed extensively throughout the `[SECTION: PREAMBLE]` (which encompasses the Business Overview and Prospectus Summary), confirming the company's strategic vision and engineering feasibility for deploying this infrastructure using its **V3 platform** and **Starship**.

Here is how the filing addresses your specific points:

**1. Orbital AI Compute Satellites & Space-Based Compute**
*   **Terminology:** The filing formally defines an *"AI compute satellite"* as a satellite equipped with onboard AI processing capabilities to perform workloads in orbit. It also defines *"orbital AI compute"* as AI computing infrastructure deployed in space, acting as orbital data centers.
*   **Strategic Vision:** The company outlines a massive long-term vision to deploy up to **100 gigawatts** of space-based compute power annually (equivalent to roughly one-fifth of total annual U.S. power production in 2025). This is framed as the only truly scalable solution to overcome Earth's finite terrestrial energy and cooling constraints, ultimately supporting the development of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) and the Grok model.

**2. Engineering Feasibility & The V3 Platform**
*   **V3 Satellite Platform:** The text notes that the core V3 satellite design is already complete and incorporates proprietary chips, providing a strong foundation for AI-focused electronics. The AI compute satellites will evolve from this platform but require significantly larger solar arrays (roughly five times the output of standard V3 designs) to generate approximately **100 kW of compute power per metric ton**.
*   **Thermal & Power Management:** The engineering feasibility relies on space-grade solar technology for unlimited, low-cost power and "radiative cooling architectures" (facing radiator panels toward cold deep space) to manage thermal loads without the operating costs of terrestrial liquid or air cooling.

**3. Starship and Launch Cadence**
*   **Starship's Role:** The deployment strategy is entirely dependent on the heavy-lift capabilities of **Starship**. The filing calculates the 100-gigawatt goal based on Starship V3 delivering **100 metric tons** to orbit per flight (with future V4 generations potentially reaching 200 metric tons).
*   **Scale and Cadence:** To achieve the 100 GW goal at 100 kW per ton, the company acknowledges it would require transporting approximately one million metric tons to orbit annually, necessitating a massive scale-up to **thousands of Starship launches per year**. 

**4. Operational Synergies (Starlink)**
*   The filing also highlights that the company will leverage its existing Starlink constellation (approx. 9,600 satellites) for automated collision avoidance, orbital debris management, and a dynamic mesh network of inter-satellite lasers to route data globally with low latency. 

In summary, the provided text serves as a comprehensive business overview of the company's pivot toward space-based AI data centers, detailing the exact hardware (V3, Starship), physics (solar power, radiative cooling), and economic rationale (bypassing terrestrial power grid limits) required to make orbital AI compute a reality.

Based on the provided excerpts from the Significant Accounting Policies and related financial notes, here is the breakdown of the Company's accounting treatments for research and development, software development, and internally developed technology:

### 1. Research and Development (R&D) Capitalization & Expensing Policies
*   **General Expensing Rule:** The Company’s baseline policy is that R&D costs are **expensed as incurred**. 
*   **Cost Components:** R&D expenses encompass a wide range of operational costs, including materials, employee and contractor compensation (including share-based compensation), cloud computing, data services, equipment leases, R&D equipment depreciation, and allocated overhead. It also includes expenses for engineering services that develop features and modules where the Company retains the intellectual property.
*   **Starship & Commercialization Trigger:** While the Starship vehicle is in the development stage, the majority of its costs are expensed to R&D as incurred. However, the policy dictates a shift to capitalization upon **commercialization** (defined as delivering payload to orbit). At that point, Starship costs will generally be capitalized and subsequently depreciated within the cost of revenue.
*   **Interest & Share-Based Compensation Capitalization:** The Company capitalizes qualified interest costs associated with significant long-term construction projects once activities to prepare the asset for its intended use commence. Additionally, a portion of share-based compensation is capitalized to the consolidated balance sheets (e.g., $154 million in 2025, $132 million in 2024, and $108 million in 2023).

### 2. Software Development Costs
*   **Technological Feasibility Threshold:** Software development costs are expensed as R&D until **technological feasibility** is established. Due to the Company's specific development cycle, this milestone typically occurs very shortly before a product is available for general release.
*   **On-Premise Perpetual Licenses:** Costs related to software marketed under on-premise perpetual license agreements are expensed.
*   **Historical Application:** Because technological feasibility is reached so late in the development cycle, **all** software development costs for the fiscal years ended December 31, 2025, 2024, and 2023 were fully expensed as incurred.

### 3. Internally Developed Technology & Internal-Use Software Accounting
*   **Internal-Use Software (FASB ASU 2025-06):** The FASB issued ASU No. 2025-06 (*Intangibles—Goodwill and Other—Internal-Use Software*), which simplifies capitalization guidance by removing prescriptive, sequential "project stages" from ASC 350-40. The Company is currently evaluating this update (effective for annual periods beginning after December 15, 2027) but does not expect it to have a material impact on its consolidated financial statements.
*   **Aerospace Hardware Classification (Reusable vs. Non-Reusable):**
    *   **Capitalized as PP&E:** Internally developed, reusable flight vehicles and components (boosters, fairings, Merlin engines, and Dragon capsules) are capitalized and classified as Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E). Satellites are also capitalized (including parts, labor, overhead, and launch costs) and depreciated over their useful lives.
    *   **Recorded as Inventory:** Non-reusable components (such as Falcon second stages) are recorded as inventory until they are launched for revenue transactions.
*   **Retained Intellectual Property:** As noted in the R&D section, costs incurred to develop internal technology features and modules through engineering services where the Company retains the IP are strictly expensed as R&D rather than capitalized as intangible assets.

Based on the provided prospectus excerpts, the forward-looking statements and risk factors regarding future satellite types, new constellations, and planned orbital infrastructure can be categorized as follows:

### 1. Future Satellite Types
The prospectus outlines the development and deployment of several next-generation satellites, which are subject to significant technical, capital, and execution risks:
*   **V3 Satellites:** Next-generation Starlink broadband satellites designed to offer 1 Tbps of downlink capacity per satellite. 
*   **V2 Mobile Satellites:** Next-generation mobile satellites designed to provide comprehensive satellite-to-mobile services, including broadband data and IoT connectivity.
*   **Orbital AI Compute Satellites:** A completely novel and unprecedented satellite type designed to host AI compute infrastructure in space. The company projects the potential deployment of these satellites as early as 2028.

### 2. New Satellite Constellations
The company's growth strategy relies heavily on the massive expansion of its Low-Earth Orbit (LEO) presence, bringing distinct operational and environmental risks:
*   **Unprecedented Scale:** Forward-looking statements indicate plans to operate "very large satellite constellations, potentially numbering up to one million satellites" to support large-scale orbital infrastructure.
*   **LEO Congestion and Collision Risks:** The continued proliferation of LEO constellations increases the probability of accidental collisions, fragmentation events, and cascading debris effects (Kessler syndrome). The company notes that its operations are vulnerable to other space actors failing to adhere to rigorous space safety, debris mitigation, and coordination practices.
*   **Space Weather Vulnerabilities:** Constellations are exposed to geomagnetic storms, solar flares, and radiation. The company warns that current radiation-hardened components and shielding may not be sufficient to prevent permanent damage or reduced performance across the fleet.

### 3. Planned Orbital Infrastructure
The company is projecting into entirely unproven markets with novel infrastructure concepts:
*   **Orbital AI Compute Infrastructure:** The company acknowledges that neither it nor anyone else has previously operated AI compute infrastructure in space. Because the harsh space environment (thermal cycles, radiation, micrometeoroids) cannot be fully replicated in testing, and because orbital hardware cannot be easily repaired or upgraded, component failures could result in permanent capacity loss and accelerated depreciation. Furthermore, the physical lifespan of the satellites is inherently shorter than the IT systems they host, requiring constant replacement launches.
*   **Lunar and Interplanetary Infrastructure:** The prospectus includes forward-looking statements regarding the size and growth of emerging markets for "lunar-related activities and interplanetary activities," noting that these ventures have lengthy time horizons and involve unprecedented risks.

### 4. Key Risk Factors & Strategic Dependencies
The deployment of these future satellites and constellations is tied to several critical risk factors:
*   **Total Dependency on Starship:** The company explicitly states that its current operational rockets (Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy) are *not* capable of deploying V3 or V2 Mobile satellites. Therefore, the entire growth strategy for next-generation constellations and orbital AI infrastructure is strictly dependent on the successful development, testing, and scaled launch cadence of the **Starship** vehicle. Any delays in Starship will materially delay or limit these deployments.
*   **Regulatory and Spectrum Hurdles:** Deploying constellations of up to one million satellites will require an unprecedented array of domestic and international approvals, including spectrum authorizations, orbital debris mitigation plans, and space situational awareness coordination. There is no assurance these will be granted on acceptable timelines.
*   **Geopolitical and Militarization Risks:** The global scale of these constellations exposes them to the increasing militarization of space. The company warns of heightened security risks, including the potential for foreign governments or malicious actors to target satellites with anti-satellite weapons or space-based warfare capabilities.
*   **Capital Expenditure vs. Near-Term Profitability:** The design, development, and deployment of these massive constellations and orbital infrastructure will require substantial, ongoing capital expenditures. Management warns that prioritizing these long-term, capital-intensive investments may result in periods of reduced profitability or increased losses. 

*Disclaimer: As noted in the prospectus's cautionary statement, these projections are forward-looking statements based on current expectations and assumptions. They involve known and unknown risks, and actual results, timelines, and market materializations may differ materially from those anticipated.*

arguments.prompt: Does the filing explicitly state the accounting treatment for orbital AI compute satellites, or whether they would be capitalized as PP&E or expensed as R&D in {{spacex_s1}}?
What is the Starship capitalization trigger and commercialization policy, and how does development stage vs commercial stage accounting work in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts, **no**, the filing does not *explicitly* state the specific accounting treatment for "orbital AI compute satellites" (i.e., it does not explicitly declare whether the satellites themselves will be capitalized as PP&E or expensed as R&D). 

However, the filing does provide explicit accounting policies for **general satellites**, **AI infrastructure**, and **early-stage development initiatives**, which strongly imply how these costs would be treated:

**1. General Satellite and Launch Costs (Capitalized as PP&E)**
*   **Satellite Hardware:** The significant accounting policies state that *"Satellites include costs to build the satellites (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as capitalized launch costs... in Property, plant, and equipment, net."*
*   **AI Segment Launch Allocations:** The filing explicitly notes that for internal launches, *"those launch costs are capitalized in satellites in Property, plant, and equipment, net,"* and states that the company expects to *"allocate a significant amount [of launch capacity] to our AI segment in the future."* 
*   **AI Infrastructure:** The notes mention that *"Construction in progress is primarily comprised of... AI infrastructure that has not yet been placed in service,"* indicating that capital expenditures for AI infrastructure are tracked as PP&E (Construction-in-Progress) before being placed into service.

**2. Early-Stage Initiatives and Development (Expensed as R&D)**
*   **Orbital AI is in Early Stages:** The Risk Factors section explicitly categorizes the initiative to *"develop orbital AI compute at scale"* as being in the *"early stages of conception, design and development"* and notes that it requires substantial ongoing investments in *"research and development."*
*   **Precedent for Development-Stage Hardware:** While not explicitly about orbital AI, the filing notes that *Starship* is currently in the development stage and, as a result, *"A majority of Starship costs are currently expensed to Research and development as incurred."* 

**Summary**
While the text stops short of explicitly writing out the exact accounting treatment for "orbital AI compute satellites" as a distinct line item, its stated policies dictate that **early-stage conception and design costs** for the initiative would be **expensed as R&D**, while the eventual **physical manufacturing and launch costs** of the satellites would be **capitalized as PP&E** (specifically within "Satellites" and "Construction-in-progress").

Based on the provided financial statements and accounting policies, here is the breakdown of SpaceX’s **Starship capitalization trigger, commercialization policy**, and how the **development vs. commercial stage accounting** works for aerospace inventory and reusable flight vehicles.

### 1. Starship Capitalization Trigger & Commercialization Policy
*   **The Trigger:** Starship will transition from the development stage to the commercial stage **once it is commercialized by delivering a payload to orbit**. The company expects to commence payload delivery to orbit in the **second half of 2026**.
*   **The Policy:** Upon reaching this commercialization trigger, Starship costs will generally be **capitalized** as Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E). These capitalized costs will then be **depreciated into Cost of Revenue** for the specific segment associated with the payload delivered. 

---

### 2. Development Stage vs. Commercial Stage Accounting
The accounting treatment for Starship shifts dramatically once the commercialization trigger is met. 

#### **Current State: Development Stage Accounting**
Because Starship is currently in the development stage, the company prioritizes rapid iteration and testing over near-term margin optimization. 
*   **Expensing as Incurred:** A majority of Starship costs are immediately expensed to **Research and Development (R&D)** as they are incurred. 
*   **What is Expensed:** R&D expenses include test flight hardware, tooling and equipment, employee compensation, allocated overhead, and depreciation for R&D equipment.
*   **Test Flight Hardware:** Specifically, **Raptor engines are expensed** when they are used in test flights, rather than being capitalized as assets. (The company notes that test flights are for data collection, so even the loss of a vehicle is considered a learning milestone, not a traditional asset impairment).

#### **Future State: Commercial Stage Accounting (Post-Trigger)**
Once Starship begins delivering commercial payloads to orbit, it will mirror the accounting treatment of the company's mature, commercialized vehicles (like Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, and Dragon).
*   **Capitalization:** Costs to build and refurbish the reusable Starship boosters, ships, and Raptor engines will be capitalized on the balance sheet as **Property, Plant, and Equipment (PP&E)**.
*   **Depreciation Method:** Unlike standard PP&E which uses straight-line depreciation over an estimated useful life, **flight vehicles are depreciated based on the expected number of average flights** for each vehicle. This depreciation is recorded directly into **Cost of Revenue**.

---

### 3. Classification, Valuation, and Depreciation Methods
The Significant Accounting Policies dictate strict rules on how aerospace hardware is classified based on its reusability:

#### **A. Reusable Components (Classified as PP&E)**
*   **Assets Included:** Falcon 9/Heavy boosters (first stages), fairings, Merlin engines, Dragon capsules, and eventually Starship boosters/ships/Raptor engines.
*   **Classification:** Classified as **Property, Plant, and Equipment, net**.
*   **Valuation & Depreciation:** Stated at cost, less accumulated depreciation. Depreciation is computed based on the **expected number of average flights** per vehicle and charged to Cost of Revenue. Significant refurbishments or enhancements that increase functionality or expected life are capitalized and depreciated ratably over the identified useful life.

#### **B. Non-Reusable Components (Classified as Inventory)**
*   **Assets Included:** Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy **second stages** (which burn up upon reentry and are not reusable), as well as raw materials, work-in-progress, and finished goods awaiting launch.
*   **Classification:** Recorded as **Inventory** until the moment of launch.
*   **Valuation:** Computed using standard cost or weighted average (approximating FIFO) and stated at the **lower of cost or net realizable value**. The company records write-downs in Cost of Revenue for estimated obsolescence or unmarketable inventories based on future demand and design changes.
*   **Revenue Recognition:** Second stages are transferred from Inventory and recorded directly to **Cost of Revenue** at the point in time they are launched (for Launch Services) or assigned (for Launch and Development contracts).

#### **C. Internal / Inter-Segment Launches (e.g., Starlink)**
*   When the Space segment launches the company's own Starlink satellites, **no inter-segment revenue is recognized**. 
*   Instead, the launch costs (including the allocation of flight vehicle depreciation and second-stage inventory) are **capitalized into the "Satellites" asset class** within PP&E, and subsequently depreciated over the satellites' useful lives in the Connectivity segment.

### Summary
In short, SpaceX treats **Starship currently as an R&D expense engine**, writing off test hardware and engines to accelerate innovation. However, the moment Starship achieves **orbital payload delivery (expected H2 2026)**, the accounting switch flips: hardware will be capitalized as PP&E, and costs will be matched against revenue via a **per-flight depreciation model**, mirroring the highly profitable, reusable economics of the Falcon 9.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a very comprehensive picture. Let me also check if there's any specific mention of how the filing discusses the accounting treatment for the AI segment's future use of launch capacity.

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about allocating launch capacity to the AI segment and how those costs would be accounted for in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided sections of the filing, here is what the document says about allocating launch capacity to the AI segment and the associated accounting treatments:

### 1. Allocation of Launch Capacity to the AI Segment
* **Future Allocations:** The company currently allocates a significant amount of its launch capacity to its Connectivity segment (primarily for deploying Starlink satellites) and **expects to allocate a significant amount to its AI segment in the future**. 
* **Strategic Purpose:** This allocated capacity is intended to support the AI segment's goal of deploying a "large fleet of orbital AI compute" (e.g., solar-powered satellites and modular orbital AI compute shells) to scale AI infrastructure beyond terrestrial limits.

### 2. Accounting Treatment for Internal Launches
* **No Inter-Segment Revenue:** The Space segment **does not recognize any inter-segment revenue** for launches dedicated to internal company purposes (such as deploying Starlink or future AI satellites). The Space segment's revenue strictly reflects *external* customer launches and customer activities.
* **Cost Capitalization:** Instead of recording a sale, the launch costs associated with these internal missions are **capitalized** as assets (recorded within *Property, plant, and equipment, net* on the consolidated balance sheet). 
* **Depreciation:** Once capitalized, these costs are **depreciated over time** within the receiving segment (e.g., the Connectivity or AI segment) rather than being expensed immediately.

### 3. Impact on the Space Segment's Financials
* **Masked Strategic Value:** Because the Space segment does not get "credit" (revenue) for launching the company's own AI and Connectivity satellites, its reported revenue scale and growth appear relatively lower compared to the rest of the company, despite an increasing overall launch cadence. 
* The filing explicitly notes that the Space segment's financial results **"do not reflect the foundational strategic value that it provides to us in bolstering the growth of our Connectivity and AI segments."** Its true value is measured by how its low-cost, high-cadence, and reusable launch capabilities enable the rapid and cost-effective deployment of the company's internal AI and satellite constellations.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a very thorough understanding. Let me synthesize the answer.

Key findings:
1. The filing does NOT explicitly resolve whether orbital AI compute satellites would be capitalized or expensed as a distinct accounting policy statement.
2. However, the filing provides several close precedents that strongly imply the treatment:
   - Existing Starlink satellites: capitalized as PP&E (build costs + launch costs), depreciated straight-line over 3-5 years
   - AI infrastructure (data centers, servers): capitalized as PP&E, depreciated over 5-6 years (servers) or 20-25 years (data center infrastructure)
   - Starship: currently in development stage → majority of costs expensed as R&D; will be capitalized as PP&E upon commercialization (payload delivery to orbit, expected H2 2026)
   - Internal launches for AI segment: the filing explicitly states launch costs will be capitalized into PP&E when allocated to the AI segment
3. The orbital AI compute initiative is described as being in "early stages of conception, design and development" — suggesting current costs are being expensed as R&D
4. The filing explicitly states the company expects to "allocate a significant amount [of launch capacity] to our AI segment in the future" and that those launch costs would be capitalized

So the answer is:
- Closest precedents: (a) existing Starlink satellite capitalization policy, (b) AI infrastructure/data center capitalization policy, and (c) the Starship development-to-commercialization transition
- The filing does NOT explicitly resolve the treatment for orbital AI compute satellites as a distinct line item
- But by inference from existing policies: development costs would be expensed as R&D, while eventual production and launch costs would be capitalized as PP&E (satellites + construction-in-progress)

arguments.final_result:
## Accounting Treatment of Future Orbital AI Compute Satellites

### Does the Filing Explicitly Resolve the Treatment?

**No.** The SpaceX S-1 does **not** contain an explicit accounting policy statement that directly resolves whether orbital AI compute satellites would be capitalized or expensed as a distinct asset class. The filing does not define a separate accounting policy line item for "orbital AI compute satellites."

However, the filing provides substantial indirect guidance through multiple disclosed policies and close precedents that, taken together, strongly imply a bifurcated treatment depending on the stage of the program.

---

### Closest Disclosed Precedents

The filing provides **three directly analogous accounting frameworks** that would govern orbital AI compute satellites:

#### 1. Existing Starlink Satellite Capitalization (Closest Hardware Precedent)
- **Policy:** Satellites are capitalized as PP&E, including build costs (parts, labor, allocated overhead) **and** capitalized launch costs.
- **Depreciation:** Straight-line over estimated useful lives of **3 to 5 years** (5 years for broadband satellites; 3 years for first-generation mobile satellites), based on engineering studies, on-orbit performance, propellant life, and planned technology transitions.
- **Inter-segment launches:** When the Space segment launches internal satellites, **no inter-segment revenue** is recognized. Instead, launch costs are directly capitalized into the "Satellites" PP&E account of the receiving segment.
- **Relevance:** Orbital AI compute satellites are physically satellites launched into orbit — the same capitalization framework would logically apply once they reach production and deployment.

#### 2. AI Infrastructure / Data Center Capitalization (Closest Functional Precedent)
- **Policy:** AI compute infrastructure is capitalized as PP&E. Specific useful lives disclosed:
  - **Servers and networking equipment:** 5–6 years
  - **Data center infrastructure:** 20–25 years
- **Construction-in-progress (CIP):** AI infrastructure not yet placed in service is tracked as CIP on the balance sheet.
- **Interest capitalization:** The filing explicitly states that interest is capitalized for "significant long-term construction projects," and specifically names **AI infrastructure data centers** as qualifying assets ($169 million capitalized in 2025).
- **Relevance:** Orbital AI compute satellites are functionally "orbital data centers" — the AI infrastructure capitalization precedent supports treating the compute payload as a capital asset.

#### 3. Starship Development-to-Commercialization Transition (Closest Stage-Gate Precedent)
- **Development stage:** While Starship is in development, **a majority of costs are expensed to R&D as incurred** (including test flight hardware and Raptor engines used in test flights).
- **Commercialization trigger:** Once Starship delivers a payload to orbit (expected H2 2026), costs will shift to being **capitalized as PP&E** and depreciated into Cost of Revenue on a per-flight basis.
- **Relevance:** The filing explicitly categorizes orbital AI compute as being in the **"early stages of conception, design and development,"** mirroring Starship's current development-stage status. This strongly implies that current and near-term orbital AI compute costs are being expensed as R&D.

---

### Implied Treatment (Filing-Supported Inference)

Based on the convergence of these precedents, the implied accounting treatment is **bifurcated by program stage**:

| Phase | Likely Treatment | Supporting Precedent |
|-------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Current: Conception, design, and development** | **Expensed as R&D** | Filing states orbital AI compute is in "early stages of conception, design and development" requiring "substantial ongoing investments in research and development." Starship development-stage precedent applies. |
| **Future: Production and deployment of physical satellites** | **Capitalized as PP&E** (within "Satellites" and/or "Construction-in-progress") | Filing explicitly states the company expects to "allocate a significant amount [of launch capacity] to our AI segment in the future" and that internal launch costs are capitalized into PP&E. Existing satellite and AI infrastructure capitalization policies apply. |
| **In-service: Operational orbital compute** | **Depreciated over estimated useful life** into Cost of Revenue (AI segment) | Analogous to Starlink satellite depreciation (straight-line, 3–5 years) and server depreciation (5–6 years). The filing notes that orbital hardware has a shorter physical lifespan than the IT systems it hosts, requiring constant replacement — suggesting a relatively short useful life. |

---

### Key Filing Quote on AI Segment Launch Allocation

The filing explicitly states:
> The company currently allocates a significant amount of its launch capacity to its Connectivity segment and **"expects to allocate a significant amount to its AI segment in the future."**

And on inter-segment accounting:
> Launch costs for internal missions are **capitalized** within *Property, plant, and equipment, net* — no inter-segment revenue is recognized.

This is the strongest direct statement in the filing about how future AI-segment orbital assets would be treated: **capitalized, not expensed**, once they reach the deployment phase.

---

### Material Disclosure Gap

The filing does **not** specify:
- A distinct useful life estimate for orbital AI compute satellites (vs. 3–5 years for Starlink or 5–6 years for servers)
- Whether the compute payload and satellite bus would be depreciated as a single unit or bifurcated into separate asset categories with different useful lives
- A specific commercialization trigger for orbital AI compute (analogous to Starship's "payload delivery to orbit" trigger)
- Whether orbital AI compute satellites would be classified within the existing "Satellites" PP&E category, "Servers and networking equipment," or a new category

This absence of explicit guidance is itself analytically material, as the orbital AI compute initiative represents a potential capital expenditure program of unprecedented scale (the filing references a long-term vision of up to 100 gigawatts of space-based compute, requiring thousands of Starship launches per year).

---

### Sources
{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

Existing satellite and AI infrastructure capitalization policies apply. |
